parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I've finally accepted that I'm just naturally bad at holding onto good things. It happens every single time something starts looking up for me. I'll get comfortable for a few days and then my own brain just decides to pick everything apart. I end up saying the wrong things or pulling back completely, and it feels completely predictable now. I just sit there watching it happen and wonder why I even bother trying to keep things steady.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're recognizing a pattern in yourself that feels frustrating and disheartening. When you say you "sit there watching it happen," it shows you're aware of the cycle, which is a meaningful step—even though it feels painful right now.

It’s human to struggle with self-doubt, especially when things start improving. That moment of comfort followed by pulling back or questioning everything is something many people experience. Your brain may be trying to protect you from disappointment, even if it ends up causing the discomfort you're trying to avoid.

You're not "naturally bad" at holding onto good things—you're navigating a pattern that has become familiar. And familiar patterns can be changed with time and understanding. It might help to explore what triggers that "picking apart" phase: Is it fear of losing the good thing? Worry about not deserving it? An old habit from past experiences?

Also, "wondering why you bother" is a sign of exhaustion with the cycle, not a sign that you shouldn't keep trying. Steadiness often comes from practicing self-awareness, like you're doing now, and allowing yourself to be imperfect in relationships or situations. It’s okay to say the "wrong thing" sometimes—authenticity includes missteps, and they don't define your worth or your ability to sustain good things.

If you'd like, we could talk about small ways to interrupt that cycle when you notice it starting. Sometimes just naming the feeling—“I’m starting to pull back again”—can give you a moment of choice instead of automatic reaction.

You're not alone in this. Many people feel like they sabotage their own happiness, but it's often a protective mechanism that can be understood and gently redirected.

turns.user_message: It's been going on for years now, and I can't seem to break the cycle no matter what I try. Every time I let my guard down, I end up making some careless mistake that ruins the whole vibe. I replay the interactions in my head until I can clearly see exactly where I went wrong. The guilt just piles up until I feel completely heavy and useless. I keep telling myself I'll do better next time, but the pattern just repeats itself over and over.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're carrying a heavy burden of self-criticism and regret. That cycle of trying, slipping up, and then replaying mistakes can be exhausting and disheartening. It takes a lot of courage to keep trying, even when it feels like you're stuck in the same loop.

It might help to remember that being human means making mistakes—sometimes the same ones more than once. That doesn’t make you useless or broken. It might mean there’s something deeper you’re trying to navigate, whether it’s a habit, a fear, or a part of yourself that’s still learning.

Would it feel okay to explore what happens right before you "let your guard down"? Sometimes noticing the triggers or feelings in those moments can help break the cycle. And if you’re open to it, speaking with someone you trust—or a professional—could offer new tools or perspectives.

You’re not alone in feeling this way, and you deserve kindness, especially from yourself.

turns.user_message: I've reached the point where I just assume I'm going to mess everything up before it even starts. It's exhausting to carry this constant weight of knowing I'm the common denominator in all my own disappointments. I watch myself sabotage the good moments and I can't even muster the energy to pretend I'm surprised anymore. The shame sits right in my chest and makes it hard to even look at what I've done. I'm just tired of being the reason everything falls apart, but I don't know how to stop it.
turns.model_response:
This is a deeply human experience you’re describing—and it’s so heavy to carry. That “common denominator” feeling can be both isolating and exhausting, especially when you feel trapped in patterns you can’t seem to change. It sounds like part of the exhaustion isn’t just from the disappointments themselves, but also from the anticipation of them, the dread that comes before anything even happens.

It might be worth acknowledging that the person observing these patterns—the one who notices the sabotage, who feels the shame—is also the person who has the potential to become aware enough to change them. Awareness can hurt, but it can also be the seed of growth.

Sometimes when we hold shame close for a long time, it starts to distort how we see ourselves. It can make us believe we’re incapable of good things or undeserving of them. But shame often comes from judging ourselves harshly for mistakes, rather than seeing those mistakes as part of being human—and as opportunities to learn, not just to condemn ourselves.

You might consider starting small. Instead of focusing on “not messing everything up,” maybe try shifting your attention to one moment at a time, one choice at a time. You don’t have to overhaul everything overnight. Sometimes just interrupting one automatic reaction—even if it’s a small one—can begin to rebuild a sense of agency.

If you haven’t already, talking to someone who can help unpack these patterns—a therapist, counselor, or trusted friend—could give you a space to explore where this cycle started and how to step out of it. It’s okay to need support; we all do, especially when we feel stuck in our own narratives.

Above all, please know: you are not the reason “everything falls apart.” You are a person who has faced disappointments, who feels pain, who wants change. That’s someone who deserves compassion—including your own.
